We will first define reference angles and their properties, and then explain how to find them for any angle.[LF][LF]### Definition and Properties[LF][LF]A reference angle is the acute angle between the terminal side of an angle and the x-axis. It is always positive and less than 90 degrees. Reference angles are useful because they allow us to find both the trigonometric function values and the signs of these functions for any angle. The reference angle is always congruent to the original angle, meaning they have the same magnitude but opposite sign. This property allows us to determine the quadrant in which an angle lies.[LF][LF]Given an angle $\theta$, its reference angle $\alpha$ can be found by taking the absolute value of $\theta$ and finding the smallest positive angle coterminal with $\theta$. In other words, if $-\pi < \theta \leq \pi$, then the reference angle $\alpha$ is given by:[LF][LF]$$[LF]\alpha = \begin{cases}[LF]\pi - |\theta|, & \text{if}\;\; \theta \in (-\pi,\ 0)\\[LF]0, & \text{if}\;\; \theta = 0\\[LF]\theta, & \text{if}\;\; \theta \in (0,\ \pi)[LF]\end{cases}[LF]$$[LF][LF]### Example[LF][LF]Let's say we want to find the reference angle for $\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$. Since $\frac{3\pi}{4} > \pi$, we need to find the smallest positive angle coterminal with it. To do this, we subtract multiples of $2\pi$ until we get a positive angle smaller than $\pi$:[LF][LF]$$[LF]\frac{3\pi}{4} - 2\pi = -\frac{5\pi}{4}[LF]$$[LF][LF]Now we take the absolute value:[LF][LF]$$[LF]|\frac{3\pi}{4}| = \frac{3\pi}{4}[LF]$$[LF][LF]Finally, we find the reference angle:[LF][LF]$$[LF]\alpha = \pi - |\frac{3\pi}{4}| = \pi - \frac{3\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}[LF]$$[LF][LF]So, the reference angle for $\frac{3\pi}{4}$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$.
